title: Password Reset (Django)
description: Add password reset to your Django app using the built-in views - most of the work is already done.

steps.title: Django's built-in reset views
steps.instruction:
## Django already built this

Unlike Flask and FastAPI where you build the token logic yourself, Django ships with a complete password reset system. It has four views, each handling one step of the flow:

| View | URL | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| `PasswordResetView` | `/accounts/password_reset/` | Displays the "enter your email" form |
| `PasswordResetDoneView` | `/accounts/password_reset/done/` | Shown after the email is sent |
| `PasswordResetConfirmView` | `/accounts/reset/<uidb64>/<token>/` | Displays the "enter new password" form |
| `PasswordResetCompleteView` | `/accounts/reset/done/` | Shown after password is updated |

### Wiring up the URLs

In your project's `urls.py`, include `django.contrib.auth.urls`:

```python
from django.contrib.auth import views as auth_views
from django.urls import path

urlpatterns = [
    # ... your existing URLs ...
    path('accounts/password_reset/', auth_views.PasswordResetView.as_view(), name='password_reset'),
    path('accounts/password_reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetDoneView.as_view(), name='password_reset_done'),
    path('accounts/reset/<uidb64>/<token>/', auth_views.PasswordResetConfirmView.as_view(), name='password_reset_confirm'),
    path('accounts/reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetCompleteView.as_view(), name='password_reset_complete'),
]
```

Or with a single include:
```python
from django.contrib.auth import urls as auth_urls
from django.urls import include, path

urlpatterns = [
    path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls')),
]
```

---

**Your turn:** Add the four password reset URL patterns to your project's `urls.py`.
steps.starter_code:
# myproject/urls.py
from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth import views as auth_views
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('todos.urls')),

    # Password reset URLs
    path('accounts/password_reset/', auth_views.PasswordResetView.as_view(), name='password_reset'),
    path('accounts/password_reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetDoneView.as_view(), name='password_reset_done'),
    path('accounts/reset/<uidb64>/<token>/', auth_views.PasswordResetConfirmView.as_view(), name='password_reset_confirm'),
    path('accounts/reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetCompleteView.as_view(), name='password_reset_complete'),
]
steps.solution_code:
# myproject/urls.py
from django.contrib import admin
from django.contrib.auth import views as auth_views
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('todos.urls')),

    path('accounts/password_reset/', auth_views.PasswordResetView.as_view(), name='password_reset'),
    path('accounts/password_reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetDoneView.as_view(), name='password_reset_done'),
    path('accounts/reset/<uidb64>/<token>/', auth_views.PasswordResetConfirmView.as_view(), name='password_reset_confirm'),
    path('accounts/reset/done/', auth_views.PasswordResetCompleteView.as_view(), name='password_reset_complete'),
]

steps.title: Email settings
steps.instruction:
## Configuring email sending

Django's password reset views send the email automatically - you just need to configure how Django sends email.

### During development: console backend

The easiest setup for development prints emails to your terminal instead of actually sending them:

```python
# settings.py
EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend'
```

With this setting, when a user requests a password reset, the email content (including the reset link) appears in your `runserver` terminal output.

### For production: SMTP

Replace the console backend with real SMTP settings:

```python
EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend'
EMAIL_HOST = 'smtp.gmail.com'         # or your email provider's SMTP host
EMAIL_PORT = 587
EMAIL_USE_TLS = True
EMAIL_HOST_USER = os.getenv('EMAIL_USER')
EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD = os.getenv('EMAIL_PASSWORD')
DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL = 'noreply@yourapp.com'
```

Store `EMAIL_USER` and `EMAIL_PASSWORD` in your `.env` file - never hardcode credentials in `settings.py`.

### The from address

```python
DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL = 'noreply@yourapp.com'
```

This is the address that appears in the "From" field of the reset email. Set it to something users will recognise.

---

**Your turn:** Add the console email backend to your settings for development.
steps.starter_code:
# settings.py (relevant section)
import os
from pathlib import Path
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
SECRET_KEY = os.getenv('SECRET_KEY', 'dev-only-insecure-key')
DEBUG = True
ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*']

# Email - console backend for development
EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend'
DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL = 'noreply@yourapp.com'

# For production, switch to:
# EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend'
# EMAIL_HOST = 'smtp.gmail.com'
# EMAIL_PORT = 587
# EMAIL_USE_TLS = True
# EMAIL_HOST_USER = os.getenv('EMAIL_USER')
# EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD = os.getenv('EMAIL_PASSWORD')
steps.solution_code:
# settings.py (relevant section)
import os
from pathlib import Path
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

BASE_DIR = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
SECRET_KEY = os.getenv('SECRET_KEY', 'dev-only-insecure-key')
DEBUG = True
ALLOWED_HOSTS = ['*']

EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend'
DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL = 'noreply@yourapp.com'
steps.hint: The settings are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Customising the templates
steps.instruction:
## Overriding Django's default templates Django's built-in views work out of the box, but they use plain, unstyled templates. You'll want to replace them with templates that match your app's design. Django looks for these four template files: | File | Used by | |---|---| | `registration/password_reset_form.html` | The "enter your email" page | | `registration/password_reset_done.html` | The "check your email" page | | `registration/password_reset_confirm.html` | The "enter new password" page | | `registration/password_reset_complete.html` | The "password updated" page | Create these in your `templates/` folder. The most important one is the email template itself: ``` templates/ registration/ password_reset_form.html password_reset_done.html password_reset_confirm.html password_reset_complete.html password_reset_email.html ← the email body password_reset_subject.txt ← the email subject ``` ### Example: password_reset_form.html ```html {% extends 'base.html' %} {% block content %}

Reset your password

{% csrf_token %} {{ form.email }}
{% endblock %} ``` `{{ form.email }}` renders the email input field. Django injects the form object automatically. --- **Your turn:** Write the password reset form template.
steps.hint: The template is already written - read through it and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Testing it end to end
steps.instruction:
## Walking through the complete flow

With the URLs, email settings, and templates in place, test the full flow:

### 1. Start the server

```bash
python manage.py runserver
```

### 2. Request a reset

Go to `http://localhost:8000/accounts/password_reset/` and enter a registered user's email address.

### 3. Find the link in the console

Because `EMAIL_BACKEND` is set to the console backend, the reset email prints in the terminal. It looks like:

```
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Subject: Password reset on localhost

You're receiving this email because you requested a password reset...

http://localhost:8000/accounts/reset/Mg/set-password/abc123.../
```

Copy the URL and open it in the browser.

### 4. Set a new password

Django's `PasswordResetConfirmView` validates the token and shows the new password form. Enter a new password and submit.

### 5. Confirm the update

The `PasswordResetCompleteView` shows the success page. Try logging in with the new password.

### What Django handles automatically

- Token generation (using HMAC with Django's `SECRET_KEY`)
- Token expiry (3 days by default, configurable via `PASSWORD_RESET_TIMEOUT`)
- Single-use tokens (each token is valid only once)
- Email sending

You get all of this without writing token logic, just by wiring up the URLs and templates.

---

**Your turn:** Run through the complete reset flow and confirm a password update works.
steps.starter_code:
# Run the development server
python manage.py runserver

# Visit the reset form:
# http://localhost:8000/accounts/password_reset/

# Check the terminal output for the reset link
# Copy the link, open it, set a new password
# Log in with the new password to confirm it worked
steps.solution_code:
# Run the development server
python manage.py runserver

# Visit the reset form:
# http://localhost:8000/accounts/password_reset/

# Check the terminal output for the reset link
# Copy the link, open it, set a new password
# Log in with the new password to confirm it worked
steps.hint: The commands are already written - run through the flow and press Check to continue.
